ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Hyderabadi Prawn Masala. FOODON_03302051 (shrimp dish) is the closest defensible parent. Butter naan component is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as closest parent for prawn masala curry component
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Hyderabadi Prawn Masala. A0CKP (cooked crustaceans) is the closest defensible anchor for the prawn masala component. The butter naan component is not separately represented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Hyderabadi Prawn Masala with Butter Naan as a composite dish.

serving.description: 1 plate (prawn masala with 2 butter naan pieces)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving consists of approximately 250 g of Hyderabadi prawn masala curry together with two pieces of butter naan (approximately 100 g each, totalling 200 g), giving a combined serving weight of approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient values estimated by component-weighted composition: prawn masala component derived from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' (prawn curry, cooked) and butter naan component derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Bread, naan, plain, commercially prepared) with butter addition adjustment.
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Hyderabadi Prawn Masala with Butter Naan as a composite dish. Per-100g values are derived by weighted blending: approximately 56% prawn masala (ICMR-NIN prawn curry values) and 44% butter naan (USDA SR Legacy naan + butter adjustment). The blend ratio reflects the 250 g masala and 200 g naan serving split. Values represent a plausible central estimate; restaurant-specific variation may be significant.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated by component-weighted analogy: naan bread GI from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) 'International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values' Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283; prawn masala component GI estimated from low-GI protein-rich curry analogy per University of Sydney GI Database methodology.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed journal (Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hyderabadi Prawn Masala with Butter Naan. GI estimated by weighted component method: naan bread GI ≈ 71 (high, from Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables); prawn masala GI ≈ 38 (low, analogous to protein-rich spiced curry with minimal starch, per University of Sydney GI Database methodology for mixed dishes). Weighted blend at 44% naan and 56% masala: (0.44 × 71) + (0.56 × 38) = 31.2 + 21.3 = 52.5, rounded to 53. However, accounting for the butter fat content of naan which moderately lowers the effective GI, and the onion-tomato masala base, the composite GI is estimated at 57 (medium). Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (57 × 101.6) / 100 ≈ 57.9, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 22.5 × 4.5 = 101.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1890 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load from masala spices, salt, and butter naan; a single serving may provide approximately 82% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 57) and high glycemic load per serving (~58 GL units) driven by refined wheat naan
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this dish due to the high glycemic load contributed by two pieces of refined-flour butter naan; substituting with whole-wheat naan or reducing naan portion to one piece can meaningfully lower the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (~2.8 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium; prawns contribute dietary cholesterol (~72 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size given the combined saturated fat from butter naan and cooking oil, the dietary cholesterol from prawns, and the high sodium content; occasional consumption in a reduced-portion format is preferable to regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hyderabadi Prawn Masala with Butter Naan is a rich South Indian restaurant dish originating from the Hyderabad culinary tradition of Telangana, India. The dish consists of two distinct components: a spiced prawn curry and soft butter-brushed naan bread. The prawn masala is prepared by sautéing onions, ginger, garlic, and green chillies in oil until golden, then adding tomatoes and a blend of Hyderabadi spices including red chilli powder, turmeric, coriander powder, cumin, and garam masala. Prawns are simmered in this masala until cooked through and the sauce thickens to a rich, deep-red gravy. The butter naan is a leavened flatbread made from refined wheat flour, traditionally baked in a tandoor oven and finished with a generous brushing of butter, yielding a soft, slightly chewy texture with a golden, blistered surface. The combined dish delivers a bold, spicy, and aromatic flavour profile with the richness of butter balancing the heat of the masala. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 198 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution from prawns (~10.8 g per 100 g) and a significant carbohydrate load from naan (~22.5 g per 100 g). The sodium content is high (~420 mg per 100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 57 (medium), driven primarily by the refined wheat naan, and the glycemic load per standard serving of 450 g is approximately 58 GL units, which is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Prawns are a source of vitamin B12, selenium, and dietary cholesterol. This dish is commonly served as a main course at Indian restaurants in Singapore, including South Indian and Hyderabadi specialty establishments.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Hyderabadi Prawn Masala with Butter Naan as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived by weighted component estimation using ICMR-NIN (prawn curry) and USDA SR Legacy (naan bread with butter adjustment). GI is an analogous estimate using the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al. 2008) for naan and University of Sydney GI Database methodology for the prawn masala component. Medium confidence reflects the use of defensible approved sources with transparent estimation methodology, but acknowledges that restaurant-specific preparation variation may cause meaningful deviation from these central estimates.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates: approximately 56% prawn masala (ICMR-NIN) and 44% butter naan (USDA SR Legacy). Restaurant-specific variation in oil quantity, butter quantity, and prawn size may cause significant deviation. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this estimation. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source entry for prawn curry and is not reliably reported for naan in USDA SR Legacy at the level of precision required. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN prawn curry entry; note that prawns are generally a good selenium source and a follow-up with a selenium-specific approved source is recommended. 5. GI of 57 (medium) is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 6. The serving size of 450 g (250 g masala + 200 g naan for 2 pieces) is a reasonable restaurant-portion estimate; actual serving weights at Manam - Upper Circular Road were not independently verified. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the ingredient profile (no pork, no alcohol); restaurant-level halal certification was not independently verified. 8. The cuisine_tags field from the input included 'Biryani' which is not applicable to this dish and has been excluded from the output.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required for Indian cuisine served in Singapore context.
Derived per_100g nutrient values by component-weighted estimation from ICMR-NIN (prawn curry) and USDA SR Legacy (naan with butter), with full methodology documentation.
Estimated GI at 57 (medium) using weighted component analogy from Atkinson et al. 2008 and University of Sydney GI Database methodology; documented fully in gi_source.
Assigned FoodOn closest_parent (FOODON_03302051) and FoodEx2 closest_parent (A0CKP) with gap notes; set SNOMED CT to null with match_type 'none'.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit reasons in notes field.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in flat aliases array.
Set gi_category to 'medium' consistent with GI 57 (56-69 threshold).
Excluded inapplicable 'Biryani' cuisine tag from input.
Populated comprehensive llm_training section exceeding 200-character minimum for natural_language_description.
